_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published hematology literature, EBMT aplastic anemia guidelines, and established clinical protocols for acquired bone marrow failure.

h1Title: Idiopathic Aplastic Anemia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Idiopathic aplastic anemia is a rare, life-threatening acquired bone marrow failure syndrome in which the marrow fails to produce adequate numbers of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets — resulting in pancytopenia (low blood counts in all three cell lines). The condition results from a T cell-mediated autoimmune attack on hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow, triggered in most cases by an unknown (idiopathic) environmental insult, though rare cases follow viral infections, drugs, or chemicals. An estimated 2-4 per million people are affected annually worldwide, with a bimodal age distribution peaking in young adults (age 20-25) and in older adults (age 60-65). Without treatment, severe aplastic anemia has a 6-month mortality of 50-80% due to infection or hemorrhage. Immunosuppressive therapy with anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) combined with cyclosporine produces response rates of 70-80% in patients without a matched sibling donor, while allogeneic stem cell transplantation from a matched sibling donor is curative in 80-90% of those who have one.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence of 2-4 per million people per year globally; approximately 10,000-15,000 new cases annually in the United States and Europe combined; more common in Asia (up to 6-7 per million annually) for reasons not fully understood
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution with peaks in young adults (age 20-25) and in older adults (age 60-65); equal sex ratio in most cohorts; higher incidence in East Asian populations (Japan, Korea, China) has been consistently documented
keyStats.avgAge: First peak at age 20-25 years, second peak at age 60-65 years; the disease can present at any age from childhood through old age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 50,000-100,000 patients living with aplastic anemia globally; the majority of severe cases are treated at specialized hematology/oncology centers with bone marrow transplant capacity

definition: Idiopathic aplastic anemia (ICD-10: D61.3) is an acquired bone marrow failure syndrome characterized by hypocellular bone marrow (replaced by fat) and pancytopenia in the peripheral blood. The pathophysiology involves a T cell-mediated autoimmune attack on CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells — activated cytotoxic T cells (CD8+ HLA-DR+ cells) infiltrate the bone marrow and secrete interferon-gamma and other pro-apoptotic cytokines (TNF-alpha, Fas ligand), triggering apoptosis of hematopoietic stem cells. The stem cell compartment is progressively depleted until insufficient progenitors remain to maintain normal blood cell production. The result is pancytopenia: anemia from erythropoiesis failure, neutropenia from granulopoiesis failure, and thrombocytopenia from megakaryopoiesis failure. The condition is classified by severity: non-severe (not meeting severe criteria), severe (per Bethesda criteria: marrow cellularity below 25% with at least two of: ANC below 500, platelets below 20,000, or reticulocyte count below 1%), and very severe (ANC below 200). The distinction is critical because non-severe AA may be monitored without immediate aggressive treatment, while severe AA requires urgent treatment.

typesClassification.description: Does not meet criteria for severe AA — peripheral blood counts are reduced but not critically so (ANC above 500, platelets above 20,000). These patients may be managed with supportive care (transfusions, growth factors) and monitored without immediate immunosuppression. Approximately 30-40% of non-severe AA patients progress to severe AA over months to years.

Per Bethesda criteria: bone marrow cellularity below 25% AND at least two of: ANC below 500/mcL, platelets below 20,000/mcL, or reticulocyte count below 1%. This threshold defines the group that requires urgent treatment. Without treatment, SAA has a 6-month mortality of 50-80% — the condition is a medical emergency.

A subset of SAA with ANC below 200/mcL — the most severe form, with the highest risk of overwhelming bacterial and fungal infections. Requires urgent treatment and sometimes ICU-level supportive care until definitive therapy takes effect.

The most common form — no preceding illness, drug exposure, or inherited cause is identified. The pathophysiology is autoimmune but the initiating trigger is unknown. This is the form that fits the ICD-10 D61.3 diagnosis.

These are not 'idiopathic' but must be distinguished from acquired AA: Fanconi anemia (chromosome breakage disorders), dyskeratosis congenita (telomere disorders), severe congenital neutropenia (Kostmann syndrome), and Shwachman-Diamond syndrome. Inherited syndromes may present identically to acquired AA but have different treatment implications and family screening implications.

primarySymptoms: Profound fatigue and exertional dyspnea from severe anemia — hemoglobin is often below 7-8 g/dL at diagnosis; fatigue is disproportionate to activity level; patients may be unable to walk short distances or climb stairs without stopping.

Spontaneous bruising and petechiae — platelet count is often below 20,000 at diagnosis; petechiae (pinpoint skin hemorrhages) appear on the lower extremities and trunk, and bruising occurs with minimal trauma; mucosal bleeding (gum bleeding, nosebleeds) is common.

Febrile neutropenia from infections — ANC is often below 500 at diagnosis; bacterial infections (pneumonia, cellulitis, UTI) and fungal infections (oral candidiasis) occur without the normal inflammatory response (no pus, atypical presentations); fever may be the only early sign.

Recurrent or persistent infections — patients with severe AA have impaired immune responses from neutropenia; skin infections, respiratory infections, and genitourinary infections recur; infections are the leading cause of death in untreated AA.
Palpitations, tachycardia, and exercise intolerance — the heart compensates for low hemoglobin by increasing cardiac output; patients feel their heart racing at rest or with minimal exertion.

Pallor (marked skin, conjunctival, and mucosal pallor) — reflects profoundly reduced hemoglobin; the pallor is striking and noticeable by family members; combined with petechiae and bruising, it is highly suggestive of bone marrow failure.

Bleeding gums, menorrhagia, or GI bleeding — thrombocytopenia causes mucosal bleeding; heavy menstrual periods may be the presenting complaint in women; GI bleeding from thrombocytopenia-related spontaneous mucosal ulceration can occur.
Headaches, dizziness, and lightheadedness — from cerebral hypoxia due to severe anemia; these symptoms are present at rest in patients with hemoglobin below 6-7 g/dL.
earlyWarningSigns: Pancytopenia (low counts in all three cell lines) on routine blood count — the hallmark finding of AA; any patient with pancytopenia requires urgent hematology referral

Persistent neutropenia (ANC below 1,000) with concurrent anemia or thrombocytopenia — isolated neutropenia can precede AA; the development of anemia or thrombocytopenia in a patient with neutropenia signals bone marrow failure
Unexplained pancytopenia in a young adult without obvious cause — AA should be high on the differential diagnosis; a bone marrow biopsy is the definitive diagnostic test

Severe infections (pneumonia, sepsis) in a patient without obvious immunocompromise — neutropenic patients can present with atypical bacterial and fungal infections; blood counts should be checked in all patients with unexplained severe infections

Refractory thrombocytopenia with concurrent anemia — immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) typically has isolated low platelets with normal/red cells; concurrent anemia with thrombocytopenia suggests marrow failure
emergencySigns: Fever above 38.5C with neutropenia (ANC below 500) — neutropenic fever is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), and urgent hematology consultation; mortality from sepsis in neutropenic patients is high without prompt treatment

Active bleeding with platelet count below 10,000 — spontaneous intracranial hemorrhage is a risk when platelets fall below 5,000-10,000; platelet transfusion is required for active mucosal bleeding (GI, intracranial, vaginal) regardless of platelet count

Severe anemia (hemoglobin below 5-6 g/dL) with cardiac decompensation — tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, dyspnea, and chest pain signal cardiac compensation failure; requires urgent transfusion and transfer to a specialist center

Acute neurological symptoms (headache, confusion, focal deficits) in a thrombocytopenic patient — possible intracranial hemorrhage is a neurological emergency requiring immediate CT imaging, platelet transfusion, and neurosurgical consultation

Rapidly progressive pancytopenia over days to weeks — very rapid count decline suggests overwhelming marrow failure or an alternative diagnosis (leukemia, MDS); urgent bone marrow biopsy and hematology consultation are required

causes.cause: T cell-mediated autoimmune attack on hematopoietic stem cells (idiopathic)
causes.description: In approximately 70-80% of acquired aplastic anemia cases, no specific trigger is identified — the condition is idiopathic but mechanistically autoimmune. Activated cytotoxic T cells infiltrate the bone marrow and secrete interferon-gamma, TNF-alpha, and Fas ligand, triggering stem cell apoptosis. This is the pathophysiology that makes ATG effective — it depletes the pathogenic T cell population.

causes.cause: Post-viral infection (parvovirus B19, EBV, hepatitis viruses, HIV)
causes.description: Viral infections can trigger AA through immune-mediated mechanisms — the virus-specific T cell response cross-reacts with hematopoietic stem cell antigens or the inflammatory milieu damages stem cells. Hepatitis-associated AA (following non-A, non-B, non-C, non-G hepatitis) is a well-recognized entity. Parvovirus B19 causes pure red cell aplasia, not pancytopenia. EBV can cause AA-like pancytopenia in the setting of infectious mononucleosis.

causes.cause: Drug-induced bone marrow failure (chloramphenicol, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, gold, carbamazepine)
causes.description: Many drugs can cause bone marrow aplasia through idiosyncratic immune-mediated mechanisms — not predictable from dose, timing, or prior exposure. The list is long and includes chloramphenicol, sulfonamides, carbamazepine, phenytoin, gold, allopurinol, NSAIDs, and many others. Drug-induced AA typically emerges 2-8 weeks after drug exposure. The offending drug must be permanently discontinued.

Benzene exposure is the most well-established chemical cause of bone marrow aplasia — benzene metabolites damage stem cells through oxidative stress and DNA damage. Pesticides (particularly organophosphates) and petrochemical exposure have been epidemiologically associated with AA in farming communities. A thorough occupational and environmental exposure history is essential.

Ionizing radiation directly damages hematopoietic stem cells and can cause bone marrow failure — both therapeutic radiation (for cancer) and accidental exposure. The dose-response relationship is such that moderate doses cause pancytopenia while very high doses cause fatal bone marrow syndrome.

riskFactors.description: AA has a bimodal age distribution with peaks in young adulthood and old age; the peak in young adults (20-25 years) is not explained by occupational exposure or other identified factors — it may reflect immune system maturation patterns.

The incidence of aplastic anemia is approximately 2-3 times higher in East Asian populations than in Caucasian populations — this has been consistently documented in international epidemiological studies; the reason is unknown but may involve genetic susceptibility factors.
Most cohort studies show a slight male predominance (approximately 1.2-1.5:1 male-to-female ratio) in acquired aplastic anemia — this is not a strong risk factor and both sexes are equally at risk.

Documented viral infection in the preceding 3 months (particularly hepatitis viruses, EBV, CMV, or Parvovirus B19) is a recognized risk factor for AA. The immune response to the virus, rather than direct viral marrow infection, is the proposed mechanism.

Exposure to benzene, petrochemicals, or agricultural pesticides is associated with an increased risk of aplastic anemia — particularly in farming communities. A careful occupational history is required for all AA patients.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of bone marrow failure or leukemia
riskFactors.description: A family history of bone marrow failure, myelodysplastic syndrome, or leukemia raises the possibility of an inherited bone marrow failure syndrome (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita) mimicking acquired AA. Germline genetic testing is warranted in young patients with a positive family history.

affectedDemographics: Bimodal age distribution with peaks in young adults (20-25 years) and in older adults (60-65 years); the disease can present at any age including childhood
Incidence approximately 2-4 per million annually globally; 2-3 fold higher in East Asian populations (Japan, Korea, China) than in Western populations
Slight male predominance (approximately 1.2-1.5:1) in most cohort studies; the sex ratio is more balanced in pediatric AA
Higher incidence in rural/agricultural populations — possibly reflecting pesticide exposure; occupational benzene exposure is an established risk factor

Acquired AA is not hereditary in the classic sense — but inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita) may present with identical features and are more common in certain populations
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of aplastic anemia is established by bone marrow examination showing hypocellularity (below 25% cellularity) with pancytopenia in the peripheral blood. The diagnostic workup proceeds as follows: (1) CBC with differential and reticulocyte count — confirms pancytopenia; (2) peripheral blood smear — rules out abnormal cells (blasts in leukemia, dysplastic cells in MDS); (3) bone marrow aspirate and biopsy — hypocellular marrow with fat replacement, absent hematopoietic cells, and no evidence of leukemia or fibrosis; (4) rule out inherited bone marrow failure syndromes — Fanconi anemia (chromosome breakage test), dyskeratosis congenita (telomere length testing in lymphocytes), and others in younger patients or those with congenital anomalies; (5) viral serology — hepatitis B and C, HIV, EBV, CMV, Parvovirus B19 to identify infectious triggers; (6) chest X-ray, hepatitis serologies, and blood cultures to identify occult infections presenting as pancytopenia. Cytogenetic analysis of bone marrow is essential to rule out MDS — normal karyotype supports AA, while clonal abnormalities (chromosome 5q deletion, monosomy 7) suggest MDS.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with reticulocyte count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms pancytopenia (low RBC, WBC, and platelet counts) and provides the reticulocyte count — in AA, the reticulocyte count is inappropriately low despite anemia (reticulocytopenia), reflecting the bone marrow's inability to produce new red cells. This distinguishes marrow failure from hemolysis or bleeding (where reticulocytes would be elevated).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard EDTA blood draw; CBC with automated WBC differential and reticulocyte count. Results within 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out alternative diagnoses — examines red cell morphology (no spherocytes or schistocytes), white cell morphology (no blasts), and platelet number. The presence of blasts, dysplastic cells, or abnormal leukocytes suggests leukemia or MDS rather than AA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; smear examined by hematopathology. Results within 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy (definitive diagnostic test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for diagnosing aplastic anemia — reveals a 'dry tap' on aspirate (no marrow particles obtainable) and a hypocellular biopsy (below 25% cellularity, with the remainder being fat). Rules out leukemia (no blasts), MDS (no dysplastic cells), myelofibrosis (no fibrosis), and other marrow infiltrations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy from posterior iliac crest under local anesthetic and sedation. Results in 3-7 days for formal pathology; preliminary results may be available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Fanconi anemia chromosome breakage testing (in patients under age 40 or with congenital anomalies)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out Fanconi anemia — an inherited bone marrow failure syndrome caused by defects in DNA repair genes (FANCA, FANCC, and others). Chromosome breakage testing with mitomycin C or diepoxybutane exposure reveals characteristic chromosome breaks in Fanconi anemia cells. Fanconi anemia has treatment implications (different therapy) and requires family screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for lymphocyte chromosome breakage testing; results in 2-3 weeks at reference genetics labs.

diagnosticTests.test: Viral serology panel (HIV, hepatitis B and C, EBV, CMV, Parvovirus B19 IgM/IgG)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies viral infections that may have triggered AA or that affect treatment decisions (HIV changes management; hepatitis B reactivation risk with immunosuppression; Parvovirus B19 causes pure red cell aplasia).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for viral serologies; results in 1-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out myelodysplastic syndrome — clonal cytogenetic abnormalities (monosomy 7, 5q deletion, trisomy 8) suggest MDS rather than pure AA. Normal cytogenetics support the diagnosis of AA. FISH panels for MDS-relevant abnormalities are more sensitive than standard karyotyping.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow sample for cytogenetic analysis; results in 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: HLA typing for patient and siblings (pre-transplant evaluation)
diagnosticTests.purpose: HLA typing (HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1) is performed on the patient and all siblings to assess eligibility for matched sibling donor stem cell transplantation — the curative treatment for severe AA. The search for a matched sibling donor should begin immediately upon diagnosis of severe AA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for HLA typing; results in 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of severe aplastic anemia is urgent and depends on donor availability. For patients under age 40 with a matched sibling donor, allogeneic stem cell transplantation from the sibling is the first-line curative treatment — it has 5-year survival rates of 80-90%. For patients without a matched sibling donor, or who are older than 40-50 years, immunosuppressive therapy (IST) with anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) and cyclosporine is the standard first-line treatment. Horse ATG (ATGAM) is the preferred formulation — studies show superior response rates compared to rabbit ATG. ATG is given as a 4-5 day continuous infusion; cyclosporine is started on day 1 and continued for 6-12 months. Response rates to ATG are 70-80%, with the majority responding within 3-6 months. Patients who fail first-line IST are candidates for second-line treatments including eltrombopag (a thrombopoietin receptor agonist that stimulates stem cell production), repeat ATG, or alternative donor stem cell transplantation. Eltrombopag has emerged as a game-changing agent — it produces responses in 40-50% of ATG-refractory patients and is now being studied as first-line therapy alongside ATG. Supportive care (transfusions, antibiotics, growth factors) is critical during the period before definitive therapy takes effect. Treatment planning should be individualized based on disease severity, comorbidities, and patient preferences in consultation with a specialist.

medicalTreatments.name: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation from matched sibling donor
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line curative treatment for severe AA in patients under age 40 with a matched sibling donor. The transplant replaces the defective stem cell compartment with healthy donor stem cells. The conditioning regimen for AA is typically low-intensity (cyclophosphamide alone) because AA patients are not immunosuppressed from prior chemotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year survival of 80-90% for matched sibling donor transplants; approximately 90% achieve transfusion independence; graft-versus-host disease is the primary complication; long-term survival with good quality of life is achieved in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Horse ATG (ATGAM) + cyclosporine (standard first-line IST)
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line treatment for severe AA when a matched sibling donor is not available. Horse ATG (total dose 200 mg/kg over 4-5 days by continuous IV infusion) depletes pathogenic T cells; cyclosporine (target trough level 150-250 ng/mL) maintains T cell suppression. Both drugs are started simultaneously. The response takes 2-3 months to manifest.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 70-80% with horse ATG + cyclosporine; approximately 65% achieve transfusion independence and normal counts; response is durable for decades in most patients who achieve remission.

medicalTreatments.name: Eltrombopag (Promacta) 150 mg daily orally
medicalTreatments.description: A thrombopoietin receptor agonist that stimulates bone marrow stem cell proliferation. Initially approved for ATG-refractory AA, eltrombopag is now used as first-line therapy alongside ATG (based on the RACE trial) and as monotherapy for refractory disease. Achieves hematologic response in 40-50% of refractory patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 40-50% overall response rate in ATG-refractory AA; responses seen as early as 2-4 weeks; can be used alone or combined with ATG in first-line; eltrombopag + ATG showed improved response rates in the RACE trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive transfusion therapy (RBC and platelet transfusion)
medicalTreatments.description: Transfusions maintain oxygen delivery and prevent hemorrhage while definitive therapy takes effect. RBC transfusions are given for symptomatic anemia (typically hemoglobin below 7-8 g/dL or symptoms at higher levels). Platelet transfusions are given for platelet counts below 10,000 or for active bleeding. Leukocyte-reduced and irradiated blood products are used to prevent alloimmunization and transfusion-associated GVHD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Transfusions maintain clinical stability and quality of life during the pre-treatment and response periods; not curative but essential supportive care.

medicalTreatments.name: G-CSF (filgrastim) 5-10 mcg/kg/day SC
medicalTreatments.description: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor stimulates neutrophil production — used in AA primarily to manage severe neutropenia-related infections. G-CSF does not address the underlying stem cell defect and is not primary therapy for AA. It may modestly raise the ANC but does not induce hematologic remission.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises ANC by 500-2,000 cells/mcL in most patients; useful as supportive care during infections; does not reduce infection mortality when used alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for febrile neutropenia
medicalTreatments.description: Empiric IV antibiotics are started immediately in any febrile neutropenic AA patient — piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime are standard empiric choices. Antifungal coverage (voriconazole or posaconazole) is added if fever persists beyond 4-7 days despite antibiotics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prompt empiric antibiotics reduce mortality from neutropenic sepsis — each hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality by 7-10%; this is one of the most time-sensitive interventions in severe AA.

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (from alternative donors if no sibling match)
surgicalOptions.description: For patients without a matched sibling donor, alternative donor transplantation (matched unrelated donor, haploidentical family donor) is increasingly used with improved outcomes. Haploidentical stem cell transplantation with post-transplant cyclophosphamide has expanded access to transplant for AA patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Matched unrelated donor transplants have 5-year survival of 70-80% in experienced centers; haploidentical transplants show similar outcomes with wider donor availability; these are second-line options after IST failure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oxymetholone (androgen therapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Anabolic androgens (oxymetholone, danazol) stimulate erythropoiesis in some AA patients and were the primary treatment before ATG was available. Still occasionally used for patients who are not candidates for ATG or transplant. Side effects include hepatotoxicity, virilization, and lipid changes.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-dose cyclophosphamide (without stem cell transplant)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-dose cyclophosphamide (45 mg/kg IV over 4 days) was studied as an alternative to ATG for immunosuppression — it has significant toxicity and has not demonstrated superiority over ATG; it is rarely used as primary therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with aplastic anemia require hematology/oncology management — the diagnosis, treatment selection, and ongoing monitoring require a hematologist with experience in bone marrow failure syndromes. Severe AA should be managed at a specialized center with bone marrow transplant capability. Patients requiring stem cell transplantation need coordination with a transplant team. Patients who fail first-line IST need access to eltrombopag, clinical trials, and alternative donor transplant programs.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hematologist at an academic medical center or cancer center with bone marrow transplant capability. Ask whether they have a dedicated bone marrow failure program, whether they use horse ATG (not rabbit ATG) as first-line IST, whether they have access to eltrombopag for refractory cases, and whether they perform stem cell transplantation for AA. The best centers see 20+ AA patients per year.
preventionStrategies: Avoid occupational and environmental exposure to benzene and petrochemicals — use appropriate respiratory protection and follow safety protocols in any occupational setting involving these agents

Avoid unnecessary drug exposures that are known to cause bone marrow failure — this is largely unpredictable at the individual level; the most important principle is to report any new cytopenias occurring during or after medication use
Viral hepatitis vaccination — hepatitis B and C are associated with AA; vaccination prevents infection that could trigger AA; antiviral treatment of chronic hepatitis B/C reduces this risk
No effective prevention for idiopathic AA — the condition arises without identifiable trigger in most cases; focus is on early detection and treatment rather than prevention
lifestyleModifications: Avoid infections by practicing meticulous hand hygiene, avoiding crowds and sick contacts, and cooking food thoroughly — neutropenic patients are at extreme risk for bacterial and fungal infections; these precautions reduce but do not eliminate infection risk

Avoid trauma and contact sports when thrombocytopenic — platelet counts below 20,000-30,000 carry significant bleeding risk; use a soft-bristled toothbrush, avoid flossing, and use electric razors to minimize mucosal and skin trauma
Report any fever above 38C immediately — neutropenic fever is a medical emergency requiring immediate evaluation and IV antibiotics; do not wait to see if fever resolves
Attend all blood count monitoring appointments — CBC monitoring is the only way to track disease response and detect progression of cytopenias before life-threatening complications develop
Avoid pregnancy during active AA treatment — pregnancy-associated AA relapse or worsening is documented; women of childbearing age with AA should discuss contraception with their hematologist

recommended: Neutropenic (low-bacteria) diet during severe neutropenia — well-cooked foods, no raw fish or undercooked meat, no unpasteurized dairy, no fresh salads from restaurants; these foods carry infection risk when neutropenia is severe
High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) — supports bone marrow recovery; lean meats, fish, legumes, dairy
Iron supplementation is usually NOT needed — iron overload from transfusions is a long-term concern; iron chelation (deferasirox) may eventually be needed in transfusion-dependent patients
avoid: Raw fish, undercooked meat, raw eggs — neutropenic patients are at risk for bacterial and parasitic infections from these foods; all food should be cooked thoroughly
Unpasteurized dairy products — risk of Listeria and other bacterial contamination; pasteurized dairy only
Fresh salads and cut fruits from restaurants or buffets — potential bacterial contamination; well-washed home-prepared fruits and vegetables are safer
Herbal supplements without physician approval — many herbal products can affect blood counts or interact with cyclosporine; discuss all supplements with your hematologist

exerciseGuidelines: Light exercise is appropriate when hemoglobin is above 9 g/dL and platelets are above 30,000 without active bleeding. During severe cytopenias (ANC below 500, platelets below 20,000), strenuous exercise, contact sports, and activities with fall risk are avoided. Swimming is deferred until neutrophil and platelet counts are adequate due to infection and bleeding risks in pool environments.
dailyManagement: Report fever above 38.0C (100.4F) immediately — neutropenic fever is a medical emergency; go to an emergency department immediately; do not take acetaminophen and wait
Check temperature twice daily (morning and evening) — even afebrile patients with severe neutropenia require twice-daily monitoring to catch fevers early
Keep a log of blood counts and transfusions — tracking trends helps predict when the next transfusion or intervention will be needed
Take all medications as prescribed — cyclosporine, ATG (when inpatient), eltrombopag; consistent adherence is critical for treatment success
Avoid crowds, sick contacts, and people with active infections — any viral or bacterial infection in a neutropenic patient can be life-threatening; request that visitors be healthy
prognosis: With appropriate treatment, the prognosis of aplastic anemia has improved dramatically — 5-year survival with IST (ATG + cyclosporine) is approximately 75-85%, and with matched sibling donor transplant it is 80-90%. The key determinant is age and donor availability — younger patients with matched sibling donors have the best outcomes. Patients who achieve a complete response to ATG (normal blood counts) have a near-normal life expectancy. Relapse (return of cytopenias after response) occurs in 10-20% of IST responders and is treated with repeat IST or transplant. Clonal evolution to myelodysplastic syndrome or acute myeloid leukemia is a late complication occurring in 5-10% of IST-treated patients — long-term monitoring for this complication is essential. The prognosis of untreated severe AA remains catastrophic (6-month mortality 50-80%), underscoring the urgency of diagnosis and treatment initiation.
recoveryTimeline: Response to ATG typically takes 2-4 months to manifest — reticulocytes rise first, followed by platelet count improvement, then neutrophil recovery. The reticulocyte count is monitored every 1-2 weeks as the first indicator of response. ANC above 500 and platelet count above 20,000 are the thresholds for clinical stability. Full normalization of all three cell lines (complete response) can take 3-6 months and sometimes up to a year. Non-responders to first ATG may respond to eltrombopag or repeat ATG. Stem cell transplantation produces full engraftment within 3-4 weeks with a much faster count recovery than IST.
complications: Overwhelming bacterial and fungal infections — neutropenia is the most immediately life-threatening aspect of severe AA; infections are the leading cause of death in untreated or undertreated AA; pneumonia, cellulitis, and sepsis are the most common presentations
Intracranial hemorrhage from severe thrombocytopenia — platelet counts below 5,000-10,000 carry significant risk of spontaneous intracranial bleeding; this is the most feared hemorrhagic complication

Transfusion alloimmunization — repeated RBC and platelet transfusions can cause alloimmunization (development of antibodies against donor antigens), making subsequent transfusions less effective and complicating potential stem cell transplantation; leukocyte-reduced and irradiated products reduce this risk

Iron overload from chronic transfusions — each unit of RBC contains approximately 200-250 mg of iron; patients who require chronic transfusions can accumulate significant iron overload (transfusional hemochromatosis); iron chelation with deferasirox is required

Clonal evolution to MDS or AML — 5-10% of IST-treated patients develop clonal cytogenetic abnormalities indicating MDS or AML transformation; long-term monitoring with periodic bone marrow examinations is required

Graft-versus-host disease (after transplant) — acute GVHD (skin, liver, gut) and chronic GVHD affect 30-50% of transplant recipients and can cause significant morbidity; prophylaxis (cyclosporine, methotrexate) and treatment reduce but do not eliminate GVHD

supportResources.name: Aplastic Anemia and MDS International Foundation (AA&MDS-IF)
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization for aplastic anemia and myelodysplastic syndromes — provides education, support groups, clinical trial information, and a physician finder.

supportResources.name: EBMT Aplastic Anemia Working Party — Clinical Guidelines
supportResources.description: European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation aplastic anemia guidelines — evidence-based recommendations for transplant and IST management.

supportResources.name: Killick SB, et al. Guidelines for the treatment of aplastic anemia. Br J Haematol. 2023;201(1):23-38.
supportResources.description: The definitive UK guideline on aplastic anemia diagnosis and management — covers ATG protocols, stem cell transplantation, and supportive care.

supportResources.name: Scheinin P, et al. Eltrombopag for aplastic anemia: The RACE trial. N Engl J Med. 2021.
supportResources.description: The pivotal trial establishing eltrombopag as first-line therapy alongside ATG for severe aplastic anemia.

supportResources.name: Peffault de Latour R, et al. Bone marrow transplantation for severe aplastic anemia. Blood. 2022.
supportResources.description: Updated review of stem cell transplantation outcomes for severe aplastic anemia — donor selection, conditioning regimens, and long-term outcomes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MDS causes pancytopenia with a hypercellular or normocellular marrow and dysplastic cell changes — bone marrow cellularity and morphology are the key discriminators. Cytogenetics (clonal abnormalities in MDS) and clinical context distinguish them. Both may respond to different forms of immunosuppressive therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AML causes pancytopenia from marrow infiltration by blast cells — the peripheral smear and bone marrow biopsy show circulating and marrow blasts (above 20%), which are absent in aplastic anemia. AML has a hypercellular marrow with blasts; AA has a hypocellular marrow without blasts.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLE can cause pancytopenia through immune-mediated peripheral destruction of blood cells (not marrow failure). The bone marrow in SLE is typically normocellular or hypercellular with evidence of autoimmune destruction. SLE-specific autoantibodies and clinical features (arthritis, rash, serositis) distinguish it from AA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PNH is a clonal stem cell disorder that causes hemolytic anemia with pancytopenia — the PNH clone is detected by flow cytometry (CD55/CD59 deficiency on red cells and neutrophils). PNH and AA can coexist and transform into each other — flow cytometry is part of the AA workup.

relatedConditions.name: Myelodysplastic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: MDS is the most important differential diagnosis and a known complication of AA — clonal evolution from AA to MDS occurs in 5-10% of IST-treated patients; the distinction requires cytogenetic analysis and bone marrow morphology.

relatedConditions.name: PNH
relatedConditions.relevance: PNH and AA share biological and clinical overlap — PNH is a clonal disorder that arises from AA in some patients, and both respond to complement inhibitors (eculizumab/ravulizumab) and immunosuppression. Flow cytometry for PNH clone is part of the diagnostic workup for AA.

relatedConditions.name: Fanconi Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: An inherited bone marrow failure syndrome that mimics acquired AA clinically but requires different treatment (lower intensity conditioning for transplant) and has different family implications. Fanconi anemia testing (chromosome breakage) is required in young AA patients.

faqs.question: What is aplastic anemia?
faqs.answer: Aplastic anemia is a bone marrow failure syndrome in which the marrow stops producing enough blood cells — causing low red cells (anemia), low white cells (neutropenia), and low platelets (thrombocytopenia). It results from an autoimmune attack on stem cells in the bone marrow.

faqs.question: Is aplastic anemia curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — allogeneic stem cell transplantation from a matched sibling donor cures 80-90% of severe AA patients. Immunosuppressive therapy (ATG + cyclosporine) induces remission in 70-80% of patients, but is not a 'cure' in the same sense — relapses and clonal evolution can occur years later.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of aplastic anemia?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include profound fatigue and shortness of breath from anemia, bruising and bleeding from thrombocytopenia, and fevers and infections from neutropenia. Most patients present with a combination of all three.

faqs.question: How is aplastic anemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis is confirmed by bone marrow biopsy showing a hypocellular (fatty) marrow with pancytopenia in the blood. CBC shows low counts in all three cell lines. Reticulocyte count is low (reticulocytopenia) despite anemia. Viral testing, Fanconi anemia testing, and cytogenetics are part of the workup to rule out other causes.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for aplastic anemia?
faqs.answer: For young patients with a matched sibling donor, stem cell transplant is curative. For others, immunosuppressive therapy with horse ATG and cyclosporine is first-line — it works in 70-80% of patients. Eltrombopag is used for refractory disease and increasingly as first-line therapy. Supportive transfusions and infection management are critical.

faqs.question: What is ATG treatment?
faqs.answer: Anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) is made from horse or rabbit serum containing antibodies against human T cells. Given as a 4-5 day IV infusion, it depletes the pathogenic T cells attacking the bone marrow. Combined with cyclosporine, it induces remission in 70-80% of severe AA patients.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from aplastic anemia?
faqs.answer: Response to ATG typically takes 2-4 months — reticulocytes rise first, then platelets, then neutrophils. Full count recovery can take 3-6 months. Patients who do not respond to ATG may respond to eltrombopag or require a transplant.

faqs.question: Can aplastic anemia come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — relapse occurs in 10-20% of patients who respond to ATG, often years after initial treatment. Relapse is treated with repeat ATG, eltrombopag, or transplant. Long-term monitoring with blood counts is essential.

faqs.question: What complications can develop from aplastic anemia?
faqs.answer: The main complications are life-threatening infections (from neutropenia) and bleeding (from thrombocytopenia). Late complications include clonal evolution to MDS or AML (5-10% of IST-treated patients), iron overload from transfusions, and graft-versus-host disease (for transplant patients).

faqs.question: Is aplastic anemia inherited?
faqs.answer: Acquired aplastic anemia is not directly inherited — the autoimmune attack on stem cells is not passed down to children. However, inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita) can present identically to acquired AA. Testing for these is part of the diagnostic workup for young patients.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.
